topics.title: Bit Manipulation
topics.description: Bit manipulation uses bitwise operators (AND &, OR |, XOR ^, NOT ~, LEFT SHIFT <<, RIGHT SHIFT >>) to operate directly on binary representations. It enables O(1) tricks that replace expensive arithmetic or data structures. Key patterns: check/set/clear/toggle a bit, find single non-duplicate using XOR, count set bits (Brian Kernighan), detect powers of two, and bitmask DP for subset enumeration.
topics.story: Every int is 32 bits. Most programmers treat numbers as decimal values. Bit-savvy programmers see the binary machinery underneath. XOR is magic: a^a=0 (anything XORed with itself = 0), a^0=a (anything XORed with 0 = itself). Finding the one unique element in a list of pairs? XOR all values — pairs cancel to 0, unique survives. It's like finding the one person without a twin by having everyone hold hands in pairs — one person is left standing.
topics.analogy: Bitwise AND = only keep bits where BOTH sides have 1 (intersection). OR = keep bits where EITHER has 1 (union). XOR = keep bits where sides DIFFER (symmetric difference). Left shift << = multiply by 2. Right shift >> = divide by 2. n & (n-1) = clear the lowest set bit (like removing the smallest denomination coin). n & (-n) = isolate the lowest set bit. Bitmask = a tiny Set<int> stored as a single integer — 32 elements in one int, constant-time operations.
topics.memoryAnchor: Check bit k: (n >> k) & 1. Set bit k: n | (1 << k). Clear bit k: n & ~(1 << k). Toggle bit k: n ^ (1 << k). Count set bits (Kernighan): while(n) n&=n-1, count++. Power of 2: n>0 && (n&(n-1))==0. XOR pairs: a^a=0, a^0=a. Get lowest set bit: n & (-n). Bitmask DP: 1<<n subsets. Integer.bitCount(n) in Java.
topics.firstPrinciples: Computers store everything as bits. Bitwise operations are single CPU instructions — the fastest possible operations. XOR has algebraic properties (commutative, associative, self-inverse) that make it useful for cancellation. Bitmasks represent sets as integers — subset enumeration via bit iteration avoids allocating Set objects.
topics.bruteForce: Finding unique element: sort and scan pairs — O(n log n). Count set bits: convert to string, count '1's — O(log n). Check power of 2: keep dividing by 2 — O(log n). Subset generation: recursive with boolean array — O(2ⁿ) with higher constant.
topics.optimizedApproach: Unique element: XOR all → O(n), O(1). Count set bits: Kernighan's n&=n-1 → O(set bits). Power of 2: n&(n-1)==0 → O(1). Bitmask for subset: 0..(1<<n)-1, each int is a subset → O(2ⁿ) with minimal overhead.
topics.whenToUse: Bit manipulation: competitive programming and system-level code. Interview: 'Find single number', 'Number of 1 bits', 'Power of two', 'Reverse bits'. Bitmask DP: traveling salesman, Hamiltonian path, subset-sum with small n. Permissions/flags: multiple boolean flags in one int. Don't use bit tricks in business logic — readability matters more there.

examples.title: Core Bit Operations — Check, Set, Clear, Toggle
examples.explanation: n & (n-1) clears the lowest set bit — because subtracting 1 flips the lowest bit and all below it. Repeating this until n=0 counts exactly the number of set bits. n & (-n) gives the lowest set bit because -n is the two's complement (~n+1) — only the lowest set bit survives the AND. These are O(1) or O(set bits) operations, far faster than string conversion.
examples.realWorldUse: Permission systems: READ=1, WRITE=2, EXEC=4 — store all in one int. Check: perms & READ != 0. Set: perms |= WRITE. Remove: perms &= ~WRITE. Java's EnumSet uses bitmask internally for enums < 64 values. Redis bitfield commands. BloomFilter uses multiple bits per element.

examples.title: XOR Tricks & Bitmask Subset Enumeration
examples.explanation: XOR cancellation: any value XORed with itself = 0. XOR all array elements — pairs cancel, unique survives. For two uniques: XOR gives a^b, find a differing bit, partition array by that bit to isolate each unique. Bitmask subset enumeration: 2ⁿ masks, each bit represents element inclusion. Works well for n ≤ 20.
examples.realWorldUse: XOR used in cryptography (one-time pad), parity checking, CRC checksums. Bitmask DP: TSP (Traveling Salesman), minimum cost to visit all nodes. Feature flags: store 32 features in one int. Java BitSet for large bitmasks.

problems.title: Number of 1 Bits (Hamming Weight)
problems.description: Write a function that returns the number of '1' bits in the binary representation of a non-negative integer n. Do NOT use Integer.bitCount().
problems.hint1: n & (n-1) clears the lowest set bit.
problems.hint2: Loop while n != 0, clearing one set bit per iteration and counting.
problems.hint3: Also works: check (n & 1), then n >>= 1 for 32 iterations.

problems.title: Single Number — XOR
problems.description: Given a non-empty array where every element appears twice EXCEPT one. Find that single element. Must be O(n) time and O(1) space.
problems.hint3: XOR all elements — pairs cancel, unique remains.

problems.title: Generate All Subsets Using Bitmask
problems.description: Given an array of distinct integers, return all possible subsets (power set) using bitmask enumeration. Do not use recursion.
problems.hint1: There are 2^n subsets for n elements.
problems.hint2: For each mask from 0 to (1<<n)-1, collect elements where bit i is set.
problems.hint3: Use (mask >> i) & 1 or (mask & (1<<i)) != 0 to check inclusion.
